preamble: The phonetic transformation from a voiced dental fricative (/ð/) to a voiceless alveolar fricative (/s/) involves both devoicing and a shift in the place of articulation from dental to alveolar. While not a universal historical sound change, this type of shift is attested in specific historical dialectal developments.

examples.note: This shift occurred in verb inflections, replacing the inherited dental fricative with an alveolar fricative. The exact mechanism (pure sound change versus analogy) has been a subject of scholarly debate.

sources: Cole, M. (2012). Old Northumbrian Verbal Morphology in the Glosses to the Lindisfarne Gospels. Universidad de Sevilla.
